elements.sent-2411-001: Strongly correlated materials exhibit complex electronic phenomena that are challenging to capture with traditional theoretical methods, yet understanding these systems is crucial for discovering new quantum materials.
elements.sent-2411-002: Addressing the computational bottlenecks in studying such systems, we present a proof-of-concept machine learning-based approach to accelerate Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) calculations.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our method predicts interacting Green's functions on arbitrary two-dimensional lattices using a two-step ML framework.
elements.sent-2411-004: First, an autoencoder-based network learns and generates physically plausible band structures of materials, providing diverse training data.
elements.sent-2411-005: Next, a dense neural network predicts interacting Green’s functions of these physically-possible band structures, expressed in the basis of Legendre polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate that this architecture can serve as a substitute for the computationally demanding quantum impurity solver in DMFT, significantly reducing computational cost while maintaining accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-007: This approach offers a scalable pathway to accelerate simulations of strongly correlated systems and lays the groundwork for future extensions to multi-band systems.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the study of condensed matter physics and materials science, understanding the electronic properties of strongly correlated materials presents a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-009: Traditional methods, such as Density Functional Theory (DFT), have proven immensely successful in describing weakly correlated systems, where the interaction between electrons can be treated as a relatively small perturbation \cite{PhysRev.136.B864, PhysRev.140.A1133 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, when it comes to strongly correlated systems, where electron-electron interactions dominate, these conventional approaches often fail to capture the essential physics, such as localization of electrons, time-dependent fluctuation effects, and quasiparticle renormalization.
elements.sent-2411-011: Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) can successfully capture the physics in these situations \cite{DMFTreview .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Nevertheless, implementing the algorithm is computationally demanding since the time and computational cost for the Monte Carlo method to converge grows with interaction strength.
elements.sent-2411-013: In our work, we aim to use machine learning techniques to speed up the process by skipping this Monte Carlo process using machine learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-014: Machine learning (ML) has been applied successfully across various challenges in condensed matter physics.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, ML techniques have been used to refine approximate solutions of the Anderson impurity model (AIM) to match exact results \cite{weber_datadriven_2021 , predict AIM spectral functions on Bethe lattices \cite{sturm_good_2021 , and extract spectral functions directly from densities of states \cite{ren_good_2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, ML has been explored for predicting Green’s functions in various representations \cite{arsenault_good_2014 and for accelerating Continuous Time Quantum Monte Carlo (CTQMC) routines \cite{song_accelerated_2019, lee_continuous_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Building on these advancements, this work introduces a novel machine learning-based method designed to accelerate CTQMC calculations on arbitrary two-dimensional lattices.
elements.sent-2411-018: By leveraging neural networks to predict interacting Green's functions, our approach provides a scalable framework for integrating ML into the DMFT workflow, addressing key computational bottlenecks and enabling faster exploration of strongly correlated systems.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{pics/DMFTalgo.pdf} \caption{Schematic overview of the DMFT algorithm. Here $\mathscr{G}_{0}$ is the non-interacting Green function, $\mathscr{G}$ is the interacting one and $\Sigma_{\sigma}$ is the self-energy.} \label{fig:DMFTalgo}
elements.sent-2411-019: DMFT simplifies the study of strongly correlated systems by mapping the original lattice problem onto a single impurity site embedded in an electron bath.
elements.sent-2411-020: This transformation reduces a complex many-body problem into a more manageable single-body problem, where the medium surrounding the impurity can be dynamically adjusted to capture the influence of the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-021: The core principle of this mapping is the self-consistency condition, which iteratively ensures that the impurity model accurately reflects the properties of the original lattice system.
elements.sent-2411-022: The impurity problem in DMFT is typically described by the Anderson impurity model, a widely-used framework for modeling the interactions between electrons at the impurity site and the surrounding medium \cite{PhysRev.124.41 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: While the general form of this model extends to multi-band cases, this paper focuses on the single-orbital, single-site Anderson impurity model with Hubbard interactions, which provides a foundational setting for developing and testing our machine learning-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-024: The action of this model can be written as \begin{equation}S = S_0 + S_U\end{equation} such that \begin{subequations \begin{equation}S_0 = -\sum_\sigma \iint_0^\beta d \tau d \tau^{\prime} d_\sigma^{\dagger}(\tau) \mathscr{G}_\sigma^0\left(\tau-\tau^{\prime}\right)^{-1} d_\sigma\left(\tau^{\prime}\right), S_U = U \int_0^\beta d \tau \ n_{\uparrow}(\tau) n_{\downarrow}(\tau),\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\beta$ is the inverse temperature, $\mathscr{G _\sigma^0$ is the non-interacting Green's function, $d_{\sigma}$ are site operators, and $U$ is the Hubbard interaction at the impurity site.}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}S_0 = -\sum_\sigma \iint_0^\beta d \tau d \tau^{\prime} d_\sigma^{\dagger}(\tau) \mathscr{G}_\sigma^0\left(\tau-\tau^{\prime}\right)^{-1} d_\sigma\left(\tau^{\prime}\right), S_U = U \int_0^\beta d \tau \ n_{\uparrow}(\tau) n_{\downarrow}(\tau),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-025: The self-consistency condition, central to DMFT, ensures that the impurity Green’s function matches the local Green’s function of the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-026: Mathematically, this is expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{SC} \mathscr{G}_\sigma\left(i \omega_n\right) =\sum_{\boldsymbol{k}} G^{\text{loc}}_{\sigma}\left(i \omega_n, \boldsymbol{k}\right),\end{equation} where \( i \omega_n \) are Matsubara frequencies, and the summation is over all momentum states \( \boldsymbol{k \) in the Brillouin zone.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Solving the impurity model yields local quantities, such as the Green’s function and the self-energy, which describe the electronic interactions within the system.
elements.sent-2411-028: These quantities are then used to update the effective medium, thereby modifying the impurity model itself (see \autoref{fig:DMFTalgo ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: This iterative process is repeated until self-consistency is achieved, ensuring convergence of the impurity and lattice properties.
elements.sent-2411-030: Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) typically relies on Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) solvers, such as continuous-time QMC (CTQMC), to compute the local Green’s function \( G^{\text{loc _{\sigma}\left(i \omega_n\right) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The quality and efficiency of these methods depend heavily on the number of Monte Carlo iterations, with computations often taking hours to days for multi-band systems.
elements.sent-2411-032: For QMC solvers, such as the Continuous-Time QMC (CTQMC), the computational time scales with both the interaction strength \( U \) and the inverse temperature \( \beta \)~\cite{DMFTreview .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In the strong-coupling regime, the time required to sample configurations and compute determinants increases due to the larger number of diagrams at higher \( U \).
elements.sent-2411-034: Similarly, lower temperatures (larger \( \beta \)) necessitate the inclusion of more Matsubara frequencies in the calculations, further increasing complexity.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the Continuous-Time Hybridization-Expansion (CT-HYB) solver, the weights for the Monte Carlo Markov chain are expressed as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} w\left(K,\left\{\alpha_j, \alpha_j^{\prime}, \tau_j, \tau_j^{\prime}\right\}\right) \equiv \operatorname{det}_{1 \leq i, j \leq K}\left[M^{-1}\right]_{i j} \cdot \operatorname{Tr} \Bigl( \mathcal{T} e^{-\beta H_{\mathrm{loc}}} \prod_{i=1}^K c_{\alpha_i}^{\dagger}\left(\tau_i\right) c_{\alpha_i^{\prime}}(\tau_i^{\prime}) \Bigr), \end{split}\end{equation} where \( M \) is the determinant of the hybridization terms, \( H_{\mathrm{loc \) is the local interaction Hamiltonian, \( \hat{c _{\alpha (\tau) \) represents the impurity operator for orbital \( \alpha \) at imaginary time \( \tau \), and \( K \) denotes the perturbation order.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} w\left(K,\left\{\alpha_j, \alpha_j^{\prime}, \tau_j, \tau_j^{\prime}\right\}\right) \equiv \operatorname{det}_{1 \leq i, j \leq K}\left[M^{-1}\right]_{i j} \cdot \operatorname{Tr} \Bigl( T e^{-\beta H_{loc}} \prod_{i=1}^K c_{\alpha_i}^{\dagger}\left(\tau_i\right) c_{\alpha_i^{\prime}}(\tau_i^{\prime}) \Bigr), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-036: At low values of \( \beta \), the computational cost is dominated by the evaluation of the trace term, which scales logarithmically with \( K \).
elements.sent-2411-037: For sufficiently large \( \beta \), however, the determinant evaluation, scaling as \( K^3 \), becomes the dominant factor.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the case of single-orbital systems, this regime is typically not reached (see Fig.~\ref{fig:scaling ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, the computational cost’s dependence on \( U \) is reduced in the CT-HYB algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-040: By contrast, in the Continuous-Time Interaction-Expansion (CT-INT) solver, the average perturbation order scales as \( \beta U \), making it less efficient in strongly correlated regimes.
elements.sent-2411-041: In the Continuous-Time Auxiliary Field approach, the perturbation order scales even more steeply, as \( \beta U^2 \).
elements.sent-2411-042: The quantum impurity solver represents the most computationally demanding step in the DMFT self-consistency loop, while the remaining steps require negligible computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-043: This bottleneck underscores the potential of ML-based approaches to significantly reduce the computational burden of DMFT by replacing or accelerating the impurity solver.
elements.sent-2411-044: In particular, we seekt to use ML techniques to accelerate this process by predicting \( G^{\text{loc _{\sigma}\left(i \omega_n\right) \) directly from the non-interacting Green’s function \( \mathscr{G ^{0 _{ \sigma \) at each DMFT step.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{scaling_U.pdf} \caption{(a) Dependence of the average perturbation order on \( \beta \), for various interaction strengths \( U \). (b) Required time in seconds for calculating the dependence on the average perturbation order. In subfigure (b), \( \beta \) grows with \( \langle n \rangle \).} \label{fig:scaling}
elements.sent-2411-045: This section introduces our machine learning approach for accelerating the impurity solver in DMFT.
elements.sent-2411-046: We describe the architecture and training process of the primary neural network, which predicts the interacting Green’s function from a given non-interacting Green’s function.
elements.sent-2411-047: To generate the necessary training data, we develop an autoencoder-based auxiliary neural network that learns the band structures of known materials and generates physically plausible two-dimensional band structures.
elements.sent-2411-048: The trained decoder of the auxiliary network serves as a generative model, providing diverse band structures from which non-interacting Green’s functions can be computed.
elements.sent-2411-049: These Green’s functions, combined with randomly sampled Hubbard interaction parameters \( U \) and inverse temperatures \( \beta \), form the dataset used to train the primary neural network.
elements.sent-2411-050: This approach ensures that the training data captures the complexity and diversity of systems relevant to DMFT, enabling accurate and efficient predictions of interacting Green’s functions.
elements.sent-2411-051: The primary neural network predicts the interacting Green’s function from its non-interacting counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-052: Starting with a random band structure, we compute the non-interacting Matsubara Green’s function \( \mathscr{G _{0 (i \omega_n) \) and its imaginary-time representation \( \mathscr{G _{0 (\tau) \) using a Fourier transform.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: These Green’s functions are defined on the interval \( [0, \beta] \), which we rescale to \( [0, 1] \) to facilitate numerical handling.
elements.sent-2411-054: The Green’s functions are then expressed in terms of Legendre polynomials~\cite{boehnke_orthogonal_2011 , as follows: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}G(\tau) = \frac{1}{\beta} \sum_{n \leq 0} \sqrt{2n+1} \, P_n[x(\tau)] G_n, G_n = \sqrt{2n+1} \int_0^\beta d\tau \, P_n[x(\tau)] G(\tau),\end{equation} \end{subequations where \( P_n \) are the Legendre polynomials (illustrated in \autoref{fig:dos ), and \( x(\tau) = \frac{2\tau {\beta - 1 \).}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}G(\tau) = \frac{1}{\beta} \sum_{n \leq 0} \sqrt{2n+1} \, P_n[x(\tau)] G_n, G_n = \sqrt{2n+1} \int_0^\beta d\tau \, P_n[x(\tau)] G(\tau),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: The Legendre coefficients \( G_n \) compactly represent the Green’s functions and serve as the input features (non-interacting Green’s function) and target outputs (interacting Green’s function) for the primary neural network.
elements.sent-2411-056: The primary neural network consists of a sequential architecture with four fully connected (dense) layers containing 64, 128, and 64 neurons, respectively, and employs ReLU activation functions for non-linearity (\autoref{fig:arch (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-057: The model is trained to minimize the Mean-Squared Error (MSE) loss, defined as: \begin{equation}\mathrm{MSE} = \frac{1}{N_{\ell}} \sum_{\ell=1}^{N_{\ell}} \left(G_{\ell} - \hat{G}_{\ell} \right)^2,\end{equation} where \( G_{\ell \) and \( \hat{G _{\ell \) are the true and predicted Legendre coefficients, respectively, and \( N_{\ell \) is the number of terms in the expansion.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: To optimize computational efficiency and reduce overfitting, the expansion is truncated to 30 coefficients due to the rapid decay of higher-order coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-059: In addition to the Legendre coefficients, the input feature vector includes a randomly generated Hubbard interaction parameter \( U \) and inverse temperature \( \beta \).
elements.sent-2411-060: By incorporating these parameters, the model captures their effects on the interacting Green’s function.
elements.sent-2411-061: This enables the network to learn a mapping from the non-interacting Green’s function, \( U \), and \( \beta \) to the Legendre coefficients of the interacting Green’s function, providing a compact and efficient prediction mechanism.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{pics/ppic1_turned.pdf} \caption{(a) Architecture of the primary neural network predicting the interacting Green's function \( G_l \) from the non-interacting \( G_l^0 \), the Hubbard interaction parameter \( U \), and the inverse temperature \( \beta \). (b) Auxiliary neural network architecture used to generate random band structures.} \label{fig:arch}
elements.sent-2411-062: To facilitate the generation of random band structures, we developed an auxiliary neural network using PyTorch~\cite{torch .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The autoencoder-based architecture, shown in \autoref{fig:arch (b), is trained on band structures extracted from known materials (\autoref{fig:auxtrain (a)).}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Once trained, the decoder serves as a generative model that produces new, physically plausible band structures from random, normally-sampled latent vectors (\autoref{fig:auxtrain (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-065: This approach enables scalability compared to directly using extracted band structures.
elements.sent-2411-066: We sourced data from the Materials Project~\cite{jain_commentary_2013 and 2DMatPedia database~\cite{zhou_2dmatpedia_2019 , selecting over 6,000 materials with explicit rectangular \( \boldsymbol{k \)-grid band structures.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: After filtering for bandwidths greater than 0.5 eV, over 1,000 materials with approximately 15,000 bands were retained.
elements.sent-2411-068: The bandwidth threshold ensures a focus on models with sparse density of states near the Fermi level.
elements.sent-2411-069: While our study emphasizes these systems, the inclusion of other band structures would be straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-070: To preprocess the data, we standardized the \( \boldsymbol{k \)-meshes by applying linear interpolation to a uniform 10-point grid along each dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The resulting two-dimensional arrays were flattened into one-dimensional vectors for input to the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-072: Although convolutional layers could encode the full two-dimensional structure, the flattening approach simplifies the model and suffices for our application, where precise spatial reconstruction is not required.
elements.sent-2411-073: The autoencoder consists of an encoder, a latent layer, and a decoder that mirrors the encoder structure.
elements.sent-2411-074: The encoder features two fully connected layers with 100 and 32 neurons, respectively, followed by a latent layer with 8 neurons.
elements.sent-2411-075: The latent layer plays a critical role in controlling the sharpness of the band structures.
elements.sent-2411-076: The decoder mirrors the encoder with layers of 32 and 100 neurons, and a symmetrization step \( X + X^T \) is applied to the output along both diagonals to preserve the physical properties of the band structures.
elements.sent-2411-077: The network minimizes the mean squared error (MSE) loss: \begin{equation}\mathrm{MSE}=\frac{1}{S_{\text{BZ}} } \sum_{\boldsymbol{k} \in \text{BZ}} \left(\epsilon_{\boldsymbol{k}}-\hat{\epsilon}_{\boldsymbol{k}}\right)^2,\end{equation} where \( S_{\text{BZ \) is the Brillouin zone (BZ) volume, \( \epsilon_{\boldsymbol{k \) is the true energy band, and \( \hat{\epsilon _{\boldsymbol{k \) is the predicted energy band.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Training was performed using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of \( 3 \times 10^{-3 \) over 100 epochs until convergence (\autoref{fig:auxtrain (c)).}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{gend_BS.pdf} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{aux_train.pdf} \caption{(a) Example of an extracted band structure from the database, (b) generated band structure, (c) learning process for different architectures.} \label{fig:auxtrain}
elements.sent-2411-079: Using the auxiliary ML model, we generate random band structures to create a training set of interacting and non-interacting Green’s functions.
elements.sent-2411-080: The target outputs (interacting Green’s functions) are computed using TRIQS~\cite{parcollet_triqs_2015 , employing the continuous-time QMC interaction-expansion solver (CT-HYB)~\cite{seth_triqscthyb_2016 with density-density interactions.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: For the simple Hamiltonian used, the solver is configured to perform 4000 cycles of 1000 iterations, with 5000 warm-up cycles.
elements.sent-2411-082: All calculations include 1025 fermionic Matsubara frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-083: This procedure yields a database of Green’s functions comprising 2000 rows, with inverse temperatures \( \beta \) ranging from 5 to 20 eV\(^{-1}\) and Hubbard interaction \( U \) values between 1.0 and 5.0 eV.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{./sample.pdf} \caption{Examples of (a) the density of states obtained from Green’s functions, (b) Green’s functions in the \( \tau \)-basis, and (c) Green’s functions in the Legendre representation. Comparisons are made for non-interacting Green’s functions (\( G_0 \)), interacting Green’s functions (\( G \)), and predicted Green’s functions (\( G_{\text{ML}} \)) for \( U = 4 \, \mathrm{eV} \) and \( \beta = 40 \, \mathrm{eV}^{-1} \).} \label{fig:dos}
elements.sent-2411-084: The dataset is divided into training, validation, and test sets in a 70:20:10 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-085: The final mean-squared error (MSE) on the test set is approximately \( 1 \times 10^{-5 \), consistent with the training and validation losses.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Predicted Green’s functions are shown in \autoref{fig:results .}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{./results.pdf} \caption{Predictions of the ML model for Green’s functions in the \( \tau \)-basis. Dotted blue: non-interacting Green’s functions (\( G_0(\tau) \)), orange: interacting Green’s functions (\( G(\tau) \)), and solid red: ML-predicted Green’s functions (\( G_{\text{ML}}(\tau) \)). The horizontal axis is imaginary time (\( \tau \)) from 0 to \( \beta \), and the vertical axis is autoscaled. Interaction strength \( U \) is in eV, and \( \beta \) is in eV\(^{-1}\).} \label{fig:results}
elements.sent-2411-087: We obtained densities of states (DOS) from Green’s functions via analytic continuation using the maximum entropy method~\cite{jarrell_bayesian_1996,silver_maximum-entropy_1990 implemented in the Maxent package~\cite{kraberger_maximum_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Compared to Padé continuation, this method avoids parameter tuning for individual samples.
elements.sent-2411-089: However, the inherent instability of analytic continuation leads to significant changes in the DOS for small errors in \( G(\tau) \).
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.99\columnwidth]{dispersion_new.pdf} \caption{(a) Dispersion of the MSE on the test set, with the line showing the average perturbation order \( \langle n \rangle \). (b) Distribution of \( \langle n \rangle \) in the test set. (c) Distribution of \( U \) in the test set.} \label{fig:errors}
elements.sent-2411-090: Interestingly, stronger interactions (\( U \)) do not result in larger errors, as shown in \autoref{fig:errors .}
elements.sent-2411-091: Lower errors are observed in regions with higher average perturbation orders \( K \), demonstrating the capability of the machine learning model to capture complex many-body physics, even in strongly correlated regimes.
elements.sent-2411-092: These trends are not merely due to the distribution of \( K \) in the training set, emphasizing the robustness of the predictions.
elements.section-2411-004: Integrating the Solver into the DMFT Framework
elements.sent-2411-093: Once trained, the ML solver was integrated into the Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) framework to accelerate the self-consistent calculations.
elements.sent-2411-094: For DMFT routines, we utilized the Two-Particle Response Function (TPRF) package of TRIQS~\cite{parcollet_triqs_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Initial results showed that a single-shot task (where the model is trained only to predict the final interacting Green’s function) delivered suboptimal results compared to a model trained on a database incorporating intermediate iterations (\autoref{fig:DMFT_both (e)).}
elements.sent-2411-096: To improve performance, we explored training the model to predict the self-energy directly in terms of Legendre polynomial coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-097: However, this approach was hindered by the slow decay of these coefficients and their constant bias in the frequency domain, which posed challenges for accurate predictions.
elements.sent-2411-098: To address these issues, an additional database incorporating intermediate iterations was built by performing full DMFT calculations on 100 sample configurations.
elements.sent-2411-099: To ensure optimal spacing of measurements and reduce autocorrelation, we employed an adaptive cycle length strategy.
elements.sent-2411-100: This approach maintained the autocorrelation time between 0.7 and 1.5 by dynamically adjusting the cycle length: doubling it when the autocorrelation time exceeded 1.5 and halving it when it dropped below 0.7.
elements.sent-2411-101: This strategy stabilized measurements and improved data quality.
elements.sent-2411-102: Each simulation was limited to 5 million measurement cycles, preceded by 3000 warm-up cycles, with an initial cycle length of 15.
elements.sent-2411-103: The Green’s functions were represented using 30 Legendre polynomials, 400 Matsubara frequencies (positive half-axis), and 4001 imaginary time points.
elements.sent-2411-104: Convergence was monitored using the difference between successive iterations of the Legendre polynomial coefficients, with a threshold of \( 5 \cdot 10^{-3 \), typically achieving convergence within 5 iterations.}
elements.sent-2411-105: To reduce noise at each iteration, the interacting Green’s function was transformed into the Legendre polynomial basis and then mapped back to Matsubara frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-106: While some samples exhibited difficulties in capturing high-frequency moments, the low-frequency self-energy generally maintained a reasonable structure.
elements.sent-2411-107: The ML model previously trained on single-shot tasks was fine-tuned on this new data.
elements.sent-2411-108: This model demonstrated convergence rates comparable to the exact DMFT method (\autoref{fig:DMFT_both (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-109: However, while the ML solver achieved convergence, its final self-energy precision was slightly lower than that obtained after a few iterations of exact DMFT.
elements.sent-2411-110: This approach shows promise for accelerating DMFT calculations by effectively skipping initial iterations.
elements.sent-2411-111: Reliable results currently require subsequent quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) iterations, but the number of iterations is reduced compared to standard DMFT (\autoref{fig:DMFT_both (e)).}
elements.sent-2411-112: Notably, even a single QMC iteration takes approximately 10 minutes on the same hardware, emphasizing the computational savings achieved.
elements.sent-2411-113: The ML solver reliably predicts the quasi-particle weight, defined as: \begin{equation}Z=\left(1-\left.\frac{\partial \operatorname{Re} \Sigma(i \omega)}{\partial i \omega}\right|_{\omega \rightarrow 0}\right)^{-1},\end{equation} which serves as an indicator of correlated behavior (values significantly larger than 1).
elements.sent-2411-114: This capability highlights the potential of the ML model for material screening (\autoref{fig:Z ).}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{pics/DMFT_compare.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{DMFT_sigma_v3.pdf} \caption{This plot shows various properties of the exact DMFT (blue) and ML solution (red) with parameters $U=2.2$ eV and $\beta = 18.1$ eV$^{-1}$. This includes: (a) Convergence of iterations. (b) Legendre polynomials. (c) Imaginary time representation. (d) Imaginary part of self-energy. (e) Imaginary part of self-energy convergence and ML result. The ML one-shot stands for ML model trained on single-shot task, ML int. steps is additionally trained on intermediate iterations. } \label{fig:DMFT_both}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{pics/Z.pdf} \caption{Exact and predicted quasi-particle weights predicted from the ML model. Inset: real part of the self-energy for one sample. Blue: extracted from the QMC solution, red: extracted from the ML model.} \label{fig:Z}
elements.sent-2411-115: In this work, we have demonstrated the feasibility of using machine learning to predict interacting Green’s functions on arbitrary 2D lattices in the single-orbital case.
elements.sent-2411-116: By leveraging dense neural networks and formulating the problem in the Legendre polynomial basis, we achieved accurate predictions of Green’s functions, highlighting the potential of machine learning for accelerating many-body physics simulations.
elements.sent-2411-117: Furthermore, we introduced an auxiliary neural network to generate random 2D band structures, enabling the creation of diverse training datasets that are scalable to more complex multi-band systems.
elements.sent-2411-118: Our approach extends to integrating the machine learning solver within the Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) framework.
elements.sent-2411-119: Fine-tuning the ML model on intermediate iterations allowed us to achieve convergence rates comparable to exact DMFT.
elements.sent-2411-120: While the self-energy predictions of the ML model were less precise than those obtained after a few iterations of exact DMFT, the solver effectively reduced the number of QMC iterations required, significantly accelerating the overall process.
elements.sent-2411-121: This hybrid approach positions the ML solver as a valuable tool for streamlining DMFT workflows.
elements.sent-2411-122: An additional strength of the ML model is its ability to reliably predict the quasiparticle weight, \( Z \), a critical parameter for understanding correlated behavior in materials.
elements.sent-2411-123: This capability positions the model as a promising tool for screening materials based on their electronic properties, particularly for identifying candidates with strong correlations.
elements.sent-2411-124: However, full precision in self-energy and other key quantities still requires subsequent QMC iterations, emphasizing the complementary nature of the ML solver rather than its replacement of traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-125: Despite these advancements, several challenges remain.
elements.sent-2411-126: The lower accuracy in self-energy predictions, particularly in strongly correlated regimes, underscores the need for further refinement of the neural network architecture and training strategies.
elements.sent-2411-127: Future work could explore hybrid approaches, incorporating both ML predictions and QMC sampling, to achieve higher precision while retaining computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-128: Additionally, extending the method to multi-band systems, finite-temperature studies, and non-equilibrium dynamics presents exciting opportunities for advancing the utility of ML in DMFT.
elements.sent-2411-129: In summary, this work establishes a foundational framework for integrating machine learning into DMFT, showcasing its potential to accelerate simulations while maintaining accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-130: The demonstrated ability to predict Green’s functions and quasiparticle weights suggests that ML-driven solvers could play a pivotal role in high-throughput material discovery, particularly in the search for novel correlated materials.
elements.sent-2411-132: Katanin for useful discussions of the DMFT algorithm and A.~A.~Khomutov for discussions regarding ML model architectures.
elements.sent-2411-133: This project started as part of the Quantum Entrepreneurship Lab course at the Technical University of Munich (TUM), led by a collaboration between PushQuantum e.V.
